behaviors.description: Clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) in a contract
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is extraction quality — whether the AI model correctly finds and categorizes every key term. This is not a module-handoff bug; it's about what the model produces. LLM-DEP: Correctness depends entirely on real LLM output. A mock would always return whatever we program it to return, hiding real extraction misses. Running 100 times on the same contract may yield different recall rates for subtle terms. REAL-INFRA/MULTI-STEP: No OS/network or multi-step dependency beyond the single AI call.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a synthetic legal contract with a known, exhaustive ground-truth set of parties, dates, obligations, and penalty clauses to the real clause extractor. Assert that every ground-truth term appears in the extractor's output with the correct category label. Failure = any ground-truth term missing or miscategorized.

behaviors.description: Risk assessor flags indirect or euphemistic problematic language under the configured risk policy
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is semantic reasoning — whether the model recognizes that a euphemism ('mutually agreed adjustment' for an uncapped liability clause) violates policy. A unit test with mocked output proves nothing. LLM-DEP: Whether 'indirect' phrasing is recognized as risky is exactly the kind of judgment that varies across real model runs and cannot be predetermined. Any mock would hard-code the answer, bypassing the entire failure surface. REAL-INFRA/MULTI-STEP: Single reviewer step, no OS/network beyond the AI call.
behaviors.test_description: Construct contract snippets where high-risk clauses are phrased euphemistically (e.g., 'reasonable cost adjustment' masking unlimited indemnification). Submit to the real risk assessor with a strict risk policy. Assert each snippet is flagged as high-risk. Failure = euphemistic clause passes unflagged.

behaviors.description: Compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is regulatory reasoning accuracy — whether the model correctly maps contract language to jurisdiction-specific rules. This is a quality/correctness question for the AI reviewer, not a module-boundary bug. LLM-DEP: Compliance determination requires legal reasoning over jurisdiction-specific regulations. Whether a given clause satisfies GDPR Article 28, for instance, depends on what the real model reasons about the text. Results can vary; mocking bypasses this entirely. REAL-INFRA/MULTI-STEP: Single step, no multi-step or OS dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare contracts with known compliance status (one fully compliant, one with a specific regulatory violation) for a target jurisdiction. Feed each to the real compliance checker. Assert the compliant contract passes and the non-compliant contract fails with the correct regulatory citation. Failure = incorrect pass/fail determination.

behaviors.description: Merge step preserves both assessments with attribution when two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is entirely in the merge function's conflict-resolution logic — given two pre-formed reviewer outputs that disagree on the same clause, does the merge preserve both with correct attribution? This is deterministic algorithmic logic, not a module-handoff failure and not LLM-dependent. LLM-DEP: No — we supply fixed, pre-constructed reviewer outputs as inputs; what the real model would produce is irrelevant to testing this specific logic. REAL-INFRA: None. The merge step operates on in-memory data structures. A unit test calling the merge function directly with crafted conflicting inputs is both necessary and sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Construct two reviewer output objects (e.g., extractor result: clause_type='standard', risk_assessor result: clause_type='high_risk') for the same clause ID. Call the merge function directly with these fixed inputs. Assert the merged report contains both assessments, each attributed to its source reviewer, with no assessment dropped or overwritten.

behaviors.description: Full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers in parallel, collecting results, merging — completes without losing any reviewer's findings
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is about the end-to-end pipeline: parallel dispatch, result collection across all three branches, and merge. Bugs here (e.g., a dropped future, a silent timeout on one reviewer, a merge that silently discards one branch's output) only appear when all steps run together. MULTI-STEP: This requires three real agentic steps running in parallel followed by a merge — a multi-step agentic sequence. LLM-DEP: All three reviewers are real AI models; their outputs must be real to expose collection and merge edge cases caused by actual response shapes. Mocking any reviewer hides whether the pipeline correctly handles real reviewer output formats and latency. A Workflow test is the minimum level that exercises the full failure surface.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full pipeline against a real contract document using all three real AI reviewers. After pipeline completion, assert: (a) the unified report contains findings attributed to all three reviewers, (b) no reviewer's output is missing from the merged report, and (c) any clause present in multiple reviewers' outputs appears with all attributions preserved. Failure = any reviewer's findings absent from the final report.
